subtopic_detail: GPA - induction immunosuppression for severe disease
stem: A 54-year-old woman is referred to your general medicine clinic with 8 weeks of nasal crusting, a saddle-nose deformity that has developed over the last month, intermittent haemoptysis, and progressive fatigue. She has lost 6 kg unintentionally. Over the past week she has noticed dark frothy urine. She has no significant past history and takes no regular medications. Examination shows nasal septal perforation, scattered bibasal crackles, and lower-limb pitting oedema to mid-shin. Blood pressure is 158/92, HR 88, SpO2 96% on room air, afebrile. Bloods show haemoglobin 92 g/L, white cell count 12.4 x10^9/L, platelets 480, creatinine 184 micromol/L (baseline 70 six months ago), CRP 88. Urinalysis shows 3+ blood and 2+ protein with dysmorphic red cells and red cell casts on microscopy. Urine protein:creatinine ratio is 320 mg/mmol. Chest CT shows multiple bilateral cavitating nodules up to 2 cm. PR3-ANCA is strongly positive at 110 U/mL (negative <20); MPO-ANCA is negative. A renal biopsy shows pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis with 60% cellular crescents.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 158/92, HR 88, SpO2 96% room air, afebrile.
data_table.Urine: 3+ blood, 2+ protein, RBC casts, uPCR 320 mg/mmol.
data_table.Imaging: CT chest: multiple bilateral cavitating nodules up to 2 cm.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate induction regimen?

options.text: Pulsed IV methylprednisolone 500 mg daily for 3 days then oral prednisolone, with weekly oral methotrexate
options.rationale: Methotrexate is reserved for induction in non-severe GPA without major organ threat (no creatinine rise, no alveolar haemorrhage). With crescentic GN and pulmonary nodules this is severe disease requiring rituximab or cyclophosphamide (KHA-CARI ANCA-Associated Vasculitis 2022; EULAR/ERA-EDTA endorsed by RACP).
source_refs: KHA-CARI Recommendations for the Management of ANCA-Associated Vasculitis 2022

options.text: Pulsed IV methylprednisolone 500 mg daily for 3 days then oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day with rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly for 4 weeks
options.rationale: Severe GPA with crescentic GN and pulmonary involvement requires high-dose glucocorticoid induction plus rituximab or cyclophosphamide. KHA-CARI 2022 favours rituximab as first-line for relapsing disease, PR3 positivity, and women of reproductive age to preserve fertility; the RAVE and RITUXVAS trials established non-inferiority. Steroid taper follows the PEXIVAS reduced-dose schedule.
source_refs: KHA-CARI Recommendations for the Management of ANCA-Associated Vasculitis 2022

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day with azathioprine 2 mg/kg/day from day 1
options.rationale: Azathioprine is a maintenance agent following induction with rituximab or cyclophosphamide, not an induction agent. Using azathioprine upfront leaves crescentic GN under-treated and risks irreversible loss of renal function (KHA-CARI 2022).
source_refs: KHA-CARI Recommendations for the Management of ANCA-Associated Vasculitis 2022

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day with mycophenolate mofetil 1 g twice daily for 6 months
options.rationale: MMF was inferior to cyclophosphamide for induction in MYCYC and is not recommended for severe GPA with renal or alveolar involvement. It is acceptable only for non-severe disease when rituximab and cyclophosphamide are contraindicated (KHA-CARI 2022).
source_refs: KHA-CARI Recommendations for the Management of ANCA-Associated Vasculitis 2022

options.text: Plasma exchange alone for 7 sessions with adjunctive oral prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg/day, deferring cytotoxic therapy
options.rationale: PEXIVAS showed plasma exchange does not reduce death or ESKD in AAV and should not be used alone. PLEX may still be considered for diffuse alveolar haemorrhage with hypoxia or coexisting anti-GBM, as an adjunct to standard induction, not in place of it (KHA-CARI 2022).
source_refs: KHA-CARI Recommendations for the Management of ANCA-Associated Vasculitis 2022

explanation.summary: Severe GPA with crescentic GN and pulmonary nodules requires induction with high-dose corticosteroid plus either rituximab or cyclophosphamide. KHA-CARI 2022 favours rituximab for PR3-AAV, relapsing disease, and women of reproductive age. Steroid taper follows PEXIVAS reduced-dose schedule. Plasma exchange is reserved for severe alveolar haemorrhage or coexistent anti-GBM, not used alone.
key_points: Severe AAV = creatinine rise, crescentic GN, alveolar haemorrhage, neurological involvement
Induction = high-dose steroid + rituximab or cyclophosphamide
Rituximab preferred for PR3, relapses, fertility preservation
PEXIVAS established reduced-dose steroid taper; PLEX is adjunctive only
Azathioprine/MMF are maintenance, not induction
explanation.pearls: Saddle-nose plus cavitating lung nodules plus crescentic GN is the textbook GPA triad; PR3-ANCA positive in ~80% (KHA-CARI 2022).

sources.label: KHA-CARI Recommendations for the Management of ANCA-Associated Vasculitis 2022

subtopic_detail: EGPA - distinguishing features and asthma context
stem: A 46-year-old man presents to your hospital clinic with 3 months of progressively poor asthma control despite escalating combination ICS/LABA and a 2-week course of oral prednisolone. Over the last 3 weeks he has developed numbness and weakness in the right foot with a foot drop, and a non-blanching purpuric rash on both shins. He has had nasal polyps removed twice and has a history of allergic rhinitis. Today he reports new pleuritic left-sided chest pain and one episode of haemoptysis (small streak). Vital signs are HR 102, BP 134/82, SpO2 94% on room air, RR 22, afebrile. Examination shows scattered wheeze, palpable purpura on shins, and right common peroneal palsy with absent dorsiflexion. Bloods show haemoglobin 134, white cell count 16.8 with eosinophils 4.8 x10^9/L (reference <0.4), platelets 360, creatinine 92 (baseline 80), CRP 64. IgE is 980 IU/mL (<100). p-ANCA/MPO-ANCA is positive at 78 U/mL. PR3-ANCA negative. Urinalysis shows trace blood, no protein. Chest CT shows patchy bilateral ground-glass infiltrates. Nerve conduction studies confirm mononeuritis multiplex.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 102, BP 134/82, SpO2 94% room air, RR 22, afebrile.
data_table.Bloods: Hb 134, WCC 16.8, eos 4.8 (ref <0.4), plt 360, creatinine 92, CRP 64.
data_table.Serology: MPO-ANCA 78 U/mL, PR3-ANCA negative, IgE 980 IU/mL.
data_table.Imaging: CT chest: patchy bilateral ground-glass infiltrates.

lead_in: Which feature most strongly supports EGPA over alternative ANCA-associated vasculitides?

options.text: MPO-ANCA positivity at 78 U/mL
options.rationale: MPO-ANCA is found in only ~40% of EGPA and is also the dominant ANCA in MPA. It is not specific for EGPA. ANCA positivity in EGPA correlates with the vasculitic phenotype (glomerulonephritis, neuropathy) rather than identifying the syndrome itself (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology).

options.text: Eosinophil count of 4.8 x10^9/L on a background of poorly controlled asthma and nasal polyposis
options.rationale: ACR/EULAR 2022 classification weights eosinophilia >=1.0 x10^9/L, obstructive airways disease, and nasal polyposis most heavily. This patient meets all three; mononeuritis multiplex and extravascular eosinophil-rich infiltrates add further score. GPA classically has nasal destruction without asthma/eosinophilia; MPA lacks both (KHA-CARI 2022; ACR/EULAR criteria).
source_refs: KHA-CARI Recommendations for the Management of ANCA-Associated Vasculitis 2022

options.text: Palpable purpura on the shins with normal renal function
options.rationale: Palpable purpura occurs across all small-vessel vasculitides including IgA vasculitis, cryoglobulinaemia, GPA and MPA. It is not discriminatory. Normal renal function is more typical of EGPA than GPA/MPA but is a supportive negative, not the diagnostic anchor.

options.text: Mononeuritis multiplex involving the common peroneal nerve
options.rationale: Mononeuritis multiplex is seen in EGPA but also in polyarteritis nodosa, GPA, MPA, RA-associated vasculitis and cryoglobulinaemia. It supports vasculitis but does not specify which.

options.text: Bilateral ground-glass pulmonary infiltrates on CT
options.rationale: Ground-glass infiltrates are non-specific and seen in GPA, MPA (alveolar haemorrhage), infection and cardiogenic causes. EGPA pulmonary infiltrates are typically transient and migratory, but the appearance alone is insufficient.

explanation.summary: EGPA is distinguished by the triad of late-onset asthma/nasal polyposis, peripheral eosinophilia >=1.0 x10^9/L, and a vasculitic phase with mononeuritis multiplex, palpable purpura, eosinophilic pneumonitis and sometimes cardiac involvement. ANCA is positive in only ~40%. The 2022 ACR/EULAR classification weights asthma + eosinophilia + nasal polyposis most heavily.
key_points: EGPA = asthma + eosinophilia + nasal polyps + vasculitis
ANCA positive in ~40%, mostly MPO/p-ANCA
Cardiac involvement is the leading cause of mortality - obtain echo and troponin
Treatment: high-dose steroid + cyclophosphamide or mepolizumab for severe disease
explanation.pearls: An asthmatic on escalating steroids who develops foot-drop, purpura and eosinophilia is EGPA until proven otherwise (KHA-CARI; eTG Rheumatology).

sources.label: KHA-CARI Recommendations for the Management of ANCA-Associated Vasculitis 2022

subtopic_detail: Takayasu arteritis - imaging and initial treatment
stem: A 28-year-old woman of South Asian background presents to your medical assessment unit with 4 months of fatigue, intermittent low-grade fever and 5 kg weight loss. Over the last 6 weeks she has developed claudication in the right arm after vacuuming, and a recent episode of transient left visual blurring lasting 10 minutes. Examination shows BP 168/96 in the left arm, unrecordable in the right arm, a right supraclavicular bruit, asymmetric radial pulses with right radial weaker than left, and a soft bruit over the abdominal aorta. There is no rash, no synovitis. She has no recent travel, no IVDU. Bloods show haemoglobin 108, WCC 8.2, platelets 460, ESR 76 mm/h, CRP 58, creatinine 72. ANCA, ANA, hepatitis serology, IGRA and blood cultures are negative. An echocardiogram is normal. The renal arteries appear normal on ultrasound. The medical registrar is considering imaging.

data_table.Vital signs: BP left arm 168/96, right arm unrecordable, HR 84, afebrile today.
data_table.Examination: Right radial pulse diminished, right supraclavicular bruit, abdominal aortic bruit.
data_table.Bloods: Hb 108, WCC 8.2, plt 460, ESR 76, CRP 58, creatinine 72.
data_table.Serology: ANCA neg, ANA neg, IGRA neg, hepatitis neg, blood cultures neg.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next diagnostic investigation?

options.text: Temporal artery biopsy of a 2 cm segment of the right superficial temporal artery
options.rationale: Temporal artery biopsy is the test for giant cell arteritis, which characteristically affects patients over 50 with cranial symptoms. A 28-year-old with upper-limb claudication and asymmetric pulses fits Takayasu (large-vessel vasculitis of the aorta and major branches), where biopsy is not feasible and imaging is the diagnostic modality (eTG Rheumatology).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology - Large vessel vasculitis

options.text: CT angiography of the aorta and major branches from arch to iliac bifurcation
options.rationale: Takayasu is diagnosed by imaging the aorta and its primary branches, demonstrating long-segment wall thickening, stenoses or occlusions in characteristic distributions (arch vessels, subclavian, renal, mesenteric). CTA, MRA or PET-CT all qualify; CTA is most accessible and rapidly demonstrates luminal narrowing plus wall thickening. ACR/EULAR 2022 classification requires imaging confirmation in a patient <60 with characteristic vessel involvement.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology - Large vessel vasculitis

options.text: Carotid duplex ultrasound alone
options.rationale: Carotid duplex can detect the macaroni sign of homogeneous wall thickening in proximal carotids, but it cannot evaluate the descending aorta, renal or mesenteric vessels which are commonly involved in Takayasu. As a sole investigation it under-stages disease extent and misses critical lesions (eTG Rheumatology).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology - Large vessel vasculitis

options.text: Catheter-based digital subtraction angiography of the aortic arch
options.rationale: Catheter angiography was the historic gold standard but is now reserved for interventional planning or when non-invasive imaging is non-diagnostic, given radiation, contrast load and arterial access risk in a young woman of reproductive age. CTA/MRA also visualises vessel wall thickening, which catheter angiography cannot (eTG Rheumatology).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology - Large vessel vasculitis

options.text: Whole-body FDG-PET without companion CT or MR
options.rationale: FDG-PET demonstrates vessel-wall inflammation but without anatomical co-registration localisation is imprecise, and luminal stenoses are not characterised. PET-CT is useful for disease activity assessment but a structural study (CTA or MRA) is the appropriate first diagnostic test (eTG Rheumatology).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology - Large vessel vasculitis

explanation.summary: Takayasu arteritis is a large-vessel vasculitis of the aorta and its major branches, typically presenting in women under 40 with limb claudication, asymmetric pulses or BP, bruits and constitutional symptoms. Diagnosis is by cross-sectional imaging (CTA, MRA or PET-CT) showing characteristic long-segment wall thickening and stenoses. Treatment is high-dose corticosteroid with a steroid-sparing agent (methotrexate, azathioprine or biologics).
key_points: Takayasu: young woman, BP/pulse asymmetry, bruits, claudication
Diagnosis = imaging of aorta + major branches (CTA/MRA/PET-CT)
Distinguish from GCA: GCA is >50, cranial, with TAB
Treatment: prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day + methotrexate or biologic; revascularise only when inflammation suppressed
explanation.pearls: Always check BP in both arms in a young woman with constitutional symptoms - a >10 mmHg difference is a red flag for large-vessel vasculitis (eTG).

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology - Large vessel vasculitis

subtopic_detail: GCA - confirmatory testing once steroid started
stem: A 74-year-old woman presents to your emergency department with a 3-day history of new right-sided temporal headache, scalp tenderness when brushing her hair, and jaw claudication after 3 minutes of chewing. She had one episode of transient monocular vision loss in the right eye lasting 5 minutes this morning. She has bilateral shoulder and hip-girdle stiffness for 6 weeks. Vital signs are HR 78, BP 142/82, SpO2 98% on room air, afebrile. Examination shows a tender, nodular right temporal artery with diminished pulsation; visual acuity is 6/6 both eyes; fundoscopy is normal. Bloods show haemoglobin 116, WCC 9.4, platelets 480, ESR 92 mm/h, CRP 96, creatinine 78. After consultation with the on-call ophthalmologist and rheumatologist, you administer IV methylprednisolone 1 g and arrange admission. The team plans temporal artery biopsy.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78, BP 142/82, SpO2 98% room air, afebrile.
data_table.Examination: Tender nodular right temporal artery, diminished pulsation. VA 6/6 bilaterally. Fundi normal.
data_table.Bloods: Hb 116, WCC 9.4, plt 480, ESR 92, CRP 96, creatinine 78.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate timing for temporal artery biopsy?

options.text: Within 24 hours of steroid initiation, prioritising urgent referral
options.rationale: While early biopsy is preferable, the diagnostic yield of TAB does not fall meaningfully in the first 1 to 2 weeks of steroid therapy because skip lesions and giant cells persist. Insisting on <24 h can lead to logistical delay of steroid (the actual sight-saving intervention) without diagnostic gain (eTG Rheumatology).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology - Large vessel vasculitis

options.text: Within 2 weeks of steroid initiation, with a minimum biopsy length of 1 cm
options.rationale: BSR and EULAR guidance, adopted in eTG, accept TAB yield as preserved within 14 days of steroid initiation. The biopsy should be at least 1 cm (ideally 2 cm) to overcome skip lesions. Critically, steroid must never be delayed waiting for biopsy when GCA is clinically suspected, because the next ischaemic event may be permanent monocular or bilateral blindness.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology - Large vessel vasculitis

options.text: After 4 to 6 weeks of steroid response to confirm the diagnosis biochemically
options.rationale: Diagnostic yield falls substantially beyond 2 weeks of steroid exposure as inflammatory infiltrates resolve. A 4 to 6 week delay would compromise histological confirmation, which is important for long-term steroid commitment and steroid-sparing decisions (eTG Rheumatology).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology - Large vessel vasculitis

options.text: Only if temporal artery ultrasound shows no halo sign or compression sign
options.rationale: Temporal artery ultrasound (halo sign, compression sign) is increasingly used and may obviate biopsy in centres with operator expertise, but where ultrasound is unavailable or equivocal, biopsy remains the standard. The decision is not contingent on a negative ultrasound (eTG; EULAR).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology - Large vessel vasculitis

options.text: Defer biopsy indefinitely if the patient improves clinically on steroid
options.rationale: Clinical response to high-dose steroid is non-specific and occurs in many inflammatory conditions. Without histological confirmation, the patient may be committed to 18 to 24 months of steroid for the wrong diagnosis, with substantial harm. Biopsy provides the anchor for long-term management (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology - Large vessel vasculitis

explanation.summary: GCA is a clinical-imaging-histological diagnosis. Glucocorticoid must be started immediately on clinical suspicion (IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 3 days if visual or neurological involvement, otherwise oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg). Temporal artery biopsy or temporal/axillary artery ultrasound should occur within 2 weeks of treatment start to preserve diagnostic yield. Biopsy length 1 to 2 cm minimises false negatives from skip lesions.
key_points: Steroid first, then biopsy - never the reverse
TAB yield preserved up to ~2 weeks after steroid start
Minimum biopsy length 1 cm (ideally 2 cm)
Temporal artery ultrasound (halo/compression) is an accepted alternative
Tocilizumab is steroid-sparing for relapsing or steroid-toxic GCA
explanation.pearls: The transient monocular blindness here is amaurosis fugax from anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy - the next event may be permanent (eTG).

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology - Large vessel vasculitis

subtopic_detail: Early RA - first-line DMARD and time-to-treatment
stem: A 38-year-old woman presents to your general medicine clinic with 10 weeks of symmetrical pain and swelling in the MCP and PIP joints of both hands, both wrists, and the MTPs of both feet. She has morning stiffness lasting 90 minutes. She is otherwise well and not pregnant; she uses condoms for contraception. Examination shows synovitis at 14 joints (8 small joint involvement). Bloods show haemoglobin 122, WCC 9.6, platelets 420, creatinine 72, ALT 28, ESR 48 mm/h, CRP 32, anti-CCP positive at 240 U/mL (negative <20), rheumatoid factor positive 96 IU/mL. Hepatitis B sAg and anti-HCV are negative. Chest X-ray is normal. Hand X-rays show periarticular osteopenia without erosions. She meets ACR/EULAR criteria for RA with a score of 8/10.

data_table.Examination: Synovitis at 14 joints; 8 small joints; symmetrical; morning stiffness 90 min.
data_table.Bloods: Hb 122, WCC 9.6, plt 420, creatinine 72, ALT 28, ESR 48, CRP 32.
data_table.Serology: Anti-CCP 240 (neg <20), RF 96, hep B sAg neg, anti-HCV neg.
data_table.Imaging: Hand XR: periarticular osteopenia, no erosions. CXR normal.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial DMARD strategy?

options.text: Sulfasalazine 1 g twice daily monotherapy with folic acid
options.rationale: Sulfasalazine is a reasonable second-line or methotrexate-intolerant choice but is less effective than methotrexate in seropositive erosive-risk disease. With high anti-CCP titre and high disease activity, methotrexate is the preferred first-line agent (Australian Rheumatology Association/eTG Rheumatology).

options.text: Hydroxychloroquine 5 mg/kg/day monotherapy with baseline ophthalmology review
options.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine is a weak DMARD reserved for mild disease, SLE overlap or as combination therapy. As monotherapy in seropositive RA with moderate-to-high disease activity it under-treats and accepts ongoing joint damage (eTG Rheumatology).

options.text: Methotrexate 15 mg weekly with folic acid 5 mg on a different day, escalating to 25 mg weekly as tolerated, plus a short prednisolone bridge
options.rationale: Methotrexate is the EULAR/ACR/Australian Rheumatology Association first-line DMARD for newly diagnosed RA. Start 10 to 15 mg weekly, escalate by 5 mg every 2 to 4 weeks to a target of 20 to 25 mg as tolerated, with folic acid 5 mg on a non-MTX day. A short tapering prednisolone bridge controls symptoms during the 6 to 12 week onset of MTX action. Window of opportunity treatment within 12 weeks prevents erosions (eTG Rheumatology).

options.text: Combination methotrexate, sulfasalazine and hydroxychloroquine triple therapy initiated simultaneously
options.rationale: Triple therapy is an option for established RA failing methotrexate monotherapy or where biologics are contraindicated/unavailable. Australian first-line is methotrexate monotherapy with escalation to combination or biologic if target not met by 3 to 6 months. Starting all three simultaneously increases toxicity without proven first-line benefit (eTG Rheumatology).

options.text: Initiate a TNF-alpha inhibitor (adalimumab) upfront without trialling conventional DMARDs
options.rationale: PBS criteria require failure of at least two conventional synthetic DMARDs (one being methotrexate at >=20 mg/week for 3 months unless intolerant) before TNF-alpha inhibitor subsidy. Upfront biologic also exposes the patient unnecessarily to infection, reactivation TB and infusion-related risks before less expensive effective therapy is trialled.

explanation.summary: Newly diagnosed RA should receive a conventional synthetic DMARD within 12 weeks of symptom onset - the window of opportunity. Methotrexate is the anchor drug: start 10 to 15 mg weekly with folic acid, escalate to 20 to 25 mg, with a short prednisolone bridge. Treat-to-target with reassessment at 3 months and switch or add if remission/low disease activity not achieved.
key_points: Treat within 12 weeks (window of opportunity)
Methotrexate is first-line; start 10 to 15 mg, escalate to 20 to 25 mg weekly
Folic acid 5 mg on a non-MTX day to reduce mucositis and cytopenias
Steroid bridge for 8 to 12 weeks while MTX takes effect
PBS requires failure of 2 csDMARDs before biologic subsidy
explanation.pearls: Anti-CCP at high titre with early erosive risk predicts aggressive disease - escalate decisively (Australian Rheumatology Association).

subtopic_detail: Methotrexate safety - pre-prescription checks and monitoring
stem: A 52-year-old man with newly diagnosed seropositive rheumatoid arthritis is to commence methotrexate. He drinks 18 standard drinks per week, has a BMI of 32, and was born in Vietnam. He has no significant past medical history and takes paracetamol as needed. Baseline bloods show haemoglobin 138, WCC 8.4, platelets 290, creatinine 78, eGFR 88, ALT 64 (reference <40), AST 52, albumin 38. Hepatitis B surface antigen is negative, anti-HBc positive, anti-HBs negative. Anti-HCV negative. HIV negative. IGRA negative. Chest X-ray is unremarkable. He has not had any vaccinations in adulthood.

data_table.Background: BMI 32, alcohol 18 std drinks/week, born Vietnam.
data_table.Bloods: Hb 138, WCC 8.4, plt 290, creatinine 78, eGFR 88, ALT 64 (<40), AST 52, albumin 38.
data_table.Serology: HBsAg neg, anti-HBc positive, anti-HBs neg, anti-HCV neg, HIV neg, IGRA neg.

lead_in: Which issue most needs to be addressed BEFORE initiating methotrexate?

options.text: Brisk alcohol reduction counselling, with a target of fewer than 4 standard drinks per week while on methotrexate
options.rationale: Reducing alcohol intake is essential and the eTG/Australian Rheumatology Association advice is no more than 4 standard drinks per week. However, this is a counselling intervention concurrent with starting therapy; it does not itself defer initiation and can be addressed alongside antiviral planning.

options.text: Workup for occult hepatitis B reactivation risk: HBV DNA and gastroenterology referral for antiviral prophylaxis
options.rationale: Isolated anti-HBc positivity in a patient from a HBV-endemic country indicates past or occult HBV infection. Methotrexate alone has low reactivation risk but when biologics or rituximab follow, reactivation can be fatal. Australasian Society for HIV, Viral Hepatitis and Sexual Health Medicine (ASHM) and gastroenterology bodies recommend HBV DNA quantification and consideration of entecavir/tenofovir prophylaxis before immunosuppression escalation. This is the highest-priority pre-treatment action.
source_refs: ASHM - Hepatitis B reactivation in immunosuppression

options.text: Repeat fasting LFTs in 4 weeks and defer methotrexate if ALT remains above 60
options.rationale: ALT 1.5x ULN in an obese drinker with metabolic risk likely reflects MAFLD or alcohol-related liver disease. eTG permits methotrexate with baseline transaminases up to 3x ULN provided monitoring is planned. Mere repetition without addressing aetiology or reactivation risk is insufficient.

options.text: Vaccinate against influenza, pneumococcal disease, COVID-19 and varicella with live and inactivated vaccines as appropriate
options.rationale: Vaccination updates are important but inactivated vaccines (flu, pneumococcal, COVID-19, HBV catch-up) can be given before or after methotrexate. Live vaccines (varicella, MMR) should be avoided once methotrexate is started, but a 2 to 4 week delay to vaccinate does not address the HBV reactivation question, which is potentially lethal (eTG; ATAGI).

options.text: Repeat IGRA in 8 weeks before initiating any DMARD therapy
options.rationale: IGRA has high specificity and a single negative result in an asymptomatic patient with a normal CXR is sufficient before starting methotrexate. Repeat testing is reserved for new exposure or for biologic initiation in high-risk patients. Repeating IGRA delays therapy without informational gain.

explanation.summary: Before starting methotrexate, screen for hepatitis B (HBsAg, anti-HBc, anti-HBs), hepatitis C and HIV; assess baseline FBC, U&E, LFTs and chest X-ray; screen for TB with IGRA if from high-prevalence country or epidemiological risk; counsel on alcohol (<4 std drinks/week), pregnancy avoidance (3 months washout before conception in both partners), and live vaccines. Isolated anti-HBc positivity flags occult HBV and requires HBV DNA and antiviral prophylaxis planning before immunosuppression.
key_points: MTX pre-checks: FBC, U&E, LFTs, hep B/C/HIV, IGRA, CXR, pregnancy
Isolated anti-HBc = occult HBV - check HBV DNA, plan antiviral cover
Alcohol <4 std drinks/week
Folic acid 5 mg on non-MTX day
Monitor FBC, U&E, LFTs at 2-4 weekly initially then 3-monthly
explanation.pearls: Anti-HBc positive patients in HBV-endemic populations are common in Australian clinics; missing this is a sentinel error (ASHM).

sources.label: ASHM - Hepatitis B reactivation in immunosuppression

subtopic_detail: RA in pregnancy - DMARD switch and flare management
stem: A 31-year-old woman with seropositive RA in remission for 18 months on methotrexate 20 mg weekly and folic acid presents to your clinic. She has been using condoms but tells you today that she and her partner want to start trying for a pregnancy. She has had no flares for 12 months and her last DAS28 was 1.8. She has no comorbidities, takes no other medications, and her last bloods 6 weeks ago showed normal FBC, UEC and LFTs. She asks how to plan this.

data_table.Current Tx: Methotrexate 20 mg weekly, folic acid 5 mg on a non-MTX day.
data_table.Disease activity: DAS28 1.8 at last review, remission 18 months.
data_table.Bloods: FBC, UEC, LFTs normal 6 weeks ago.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate pre-conception medication plan?

options.text: Cease methotrexate immediately, wait 1 month, then attempt conception while monitoring for flare
options.rationale: Methotrexate is teratogenic and abortifacient. Australian Rheumatology Association and eTG advise a washout of at least 1 to 3 months before attempting conception (UK BSR uses 3 months; some current guidelines accept 1 month given short half-life). However, ceasing without a replacement DMARD risks flare during conception. Both partners should have a washout discussion (eTG Rheumatology).

options.text: Cease methotrexate, switch to hydroxychloroquine 5 mg/kg/day with or without sulfasalazine, and attempt conception after a 1 to 3 month washout
options.rationale: Methotrexate must be stopped at least 1 to 3 months before conception. Bridge to pregnancy-compatible DMARDs - hydroxychloroquine and/or sulfasalazine - which can be continued throughout pregnancy and lactation. This maintains disease control, reduces flare risk and avoids the foetal harm of MTX (eTG Rheumatology; ARA Position Statement).
source_refs: Australian Rheumatology Association - Medication in pregnancy

options.text: Continue methotrexate until pregnancy is confirmed, then cease immediately
options.rationale: Methotrexate exposure in early embryogenesis causes neural tube defects, craniofacial abnormalities and limb defects, and is abortifacient. By the time pregnancy is confirmed (5 to 6 weeks), exposure has already occurred during organogenesis. This approach causes the harm it is supposed to avoid (eTG).

options.text: Cease methotrexate and switch to leflunomide as a steroid-sparing alternative
options.rationale: Leflunomide is teratogenic with an active metabolite that persists for up to 2 years. Cholestyramine washout is required before conception, and it is contraindicated in women planning pregnancy. This is a worse choice than continuing methotrexate (eTG).

options.text: Continue methotrexate and add high-dose folic acid 5 mg daily to mitigate foetal harm
options.rationale: Folic acid does not abrogate methotrexate teratogenicity. Methotrexate is contraindicated in pregnancy regardless of folic acid dose. This is a common but dangerous misconception (eTG).

explanation.summary: Methotrexate is contraindicated in pregnancy and must be stopped 1 to 3 months before conception in both partners. Bridge to pregnancy-compatible DMARDs - hydroxychloroquine and sulfasalazine - which can also be continued during lactation. Certolizumab pegol is the TNF-inhibitor of choice in pregnancy due to minimal placental transfer. Leflunomide requires cholestyramine washout. RA tends to improve in pregnancy and flare postpartum, so plan postnatal escalation.
key_points: MTX: cease 1-3 months pre-conception in both partners
Pregnancy-compatible DMARDs: hydroxychloroquine, sulfasalazine
Pregnancy-compatible biologic: certolizumab pegol
Avoid: leflunomide (2-year half-life), MMF, cyclophosphamide
Plan postpartum flare review and lactation-compatible medication
explanation.pearls: Always counsel both partners; paternal MTX exposure also requires washout per ARA position statement.

sources.label: Australian Rheumatology Association - Medication in pregnancy

subtopic_detail: Psoriatic arthritis - pattern recognition and first-line therapy
stem: A 44-year-old man presents to your general medicine clinic with 4 months of progressive pain and swelling in his right index DIP and left great toe DIP, with sausage swelling of the entire 3rd left toe. He has plaque psoriasis on his scalp and elbows for 10 years, managed with topical calcipotriol/betamethasone, and pitting of several nails. There is no axial pain. Examination shows synovitis at the right 2nd DIP and left great toe DIP, dactylitis of the left 3rd toe, and three nail-pits on the left thumb. Bloods show normal FBC, UEC, LFTs; ESR 28, CRP 18; RF and anti-CCP negative; HLA-B27 not tested. Hand and foot X-rays show pencil-in-cup deformity at the right 2nd DIP and juxta-articular new bone formation at the left 1st DIP. There is no axial involvement on examination.

data_table.Examination: DIP synovitis right 2nd, left 1st toe; dactylitis left 3rd toe; nail pits; psoriasis scalp/elbows.
data_table.Bloods: Normal FBC/UEC/LFTs; ESR 28, CRP 18; RF neg, anti-CCP neg.
data_table.Imaging: Pencil-in-cup right 2nd DIP; juxta-articular new bone left 1st DIP.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line systemic DMARD?

options.text: Methotrexate 15 mg weekly with folic acid, escalating as tolerated
options.rationale: For peripheral psoriatic arthritis with synovitis, dactylitis and nail involvement and no contraindications, methotrexate is first-line conventional DMARD per eTG/Australasian guidance. It also treats cutaneous psoriasis. Escalate to 20 to 25 mg weekly with folic acid. TNF-alpha inhibitors and IL-17 inhibitors are reserved for csDMARD failure (PBS criteria require this for subsidy).

options.text: Sulfasalazine 1 g twice daily, escalating to 2 g twice daily
options.rationale: Sulfasalazine is effective for peripheral joint disease in PsA but does not treat skin psoriasis, dactylitis or enthesitis. With prominent skin disease and dactylitis, methotrexate is preferred first-line. Sulfasalazine is reasonable if methotrexate intolerant (eTG Rheumatology).

options.text: Adalimumab 40 mg subcutaneously every 2 weeks as first-line therapy
options.rationale: TNF-alpha inhibitors are highly effective in PsA but PBS subsidy requires prior failure of at least 6 months of methotrexate or leflunomide (or contraindication). Upfront biologic is not standard first-line for peripheral disease without poor prognostic markers requiring rapid escalation (PBS).

options.text: Hydroxychloroquine 5 mg/kg/day as a steroid-sparing agent
options.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine is relatively contraindicated in psoriasis due to risk of triggering generalised pustular psoriasis or erythroderma. It is not recommended in PsA (eTG; ARA).

options.text: Oral prednisolone 15 mg daily long-term as monotherapy
options.rationale: Long-term oral steroid is contraindicated in psoriasis as withdrawal can precipitate generalised pustular psoriasis. Short bridging tapers can be used cautiously while a DMARD takes effect, but never as monotherapy (eTG).

explanation.summary: Psoriatic arthritis presents with peripheral synovitis (especially DIPs), dactylitis (sausage digit), enthesitis, nail changes (pitting, onycholysis) and the radiographic pencil-in-cup with juxta-articular new bone. RF/anti-CCP are typically negative. First-line systemic therapy is methotrexate; escalate to TNF-alpha, IL-17 or IL-23 inhibitor per PBS criteria if inadequate response at 3 to 6 months.
key_points: PsA = DIP synovitis + dactylitis + enthesitis + nail changes
RF/anti-CCP negative; pencil-in-cup classic on imaging
Methotrexate first-line; sulfasalazine if MTX intolerant
Avoid hydroxychloroquine and long-term oral steroid (psoriasis flare)
PBS biologic after >=6 months MTX or leflunomide failure
explanation.pearls: Sausage digit + DIP involvement + psoriasis = PsA, even with normal inflammatory markers (eTG).

subtopic_detail: Systemic sclerosis - early detection of renal crisis
stem: A 56-year-old woman with diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis (anti-RNA polymerase III positive) diagnosed 14 months ago presents to your emergency department with 5 days of progressive headache and visual blurring, and a new dry cough. She is on prednisolone 25 mg daily commenced 3 weeks ago for myositis overlap, plus mycophenolate 1 g twice daily, omeprazole, and amlodipine 5 mg. She has no chest pain. Vital signs are BP 198/122, HR 102, SpO2 96% on room air, afebrile. Examination shows widespread skin tightening, bibasal crackles, normal heart sounds, and grade III hypertensive retinopathy on fundoscopy. Bloods show haemoglobin 88 g/L (was 122 a month ago) with schistocytes on film, platelets 78 (was 240), creatinine 286 (baseline 78), LDH 1850, haptoglobin <0.1, direct Coombs negative, ADAMTS13 activity 65%. Urinalysis 2+ protein, 1+ blood. CT brain is unremarkable.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 198/122, HR 102, SpO2 96% room air, afebrile.
data_table.Examination: Tight skin, bibasal crackles, grade III hypertensive retinopathy.
data_table.Bloods: Hb 88 (was 122), schistocytes+, plt 78 (was 240), creatinine 286 (baseline 78), LDH 1850, haptoglobin <0.1, Coombs neg, ADAMTS13 65%.
data_table.Background: Diffuse SSc, RNA-pol III+, prednisolone 25 mg/day x 3 weeks.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate treatment?

options.text: IV hydralazine and IV labetalol titrated to a 25% MAP reduction over 1 hour
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors are the disease-modifying treatment for scleroderma renal crisis. Other antihypertensives reduce blood pressure but do not reverse the renin-angiotensin-driven thrombotic microangiopathy. ACEi is the only therapy that improves renal and patient survival in this condition (eTG Rheumatology).

options.text: Oral captopril 12.5 mg starting dose, uptitrated 6 to 8 hourly to achieve BP control even at the expense of further short-term creatinine rise
options.rationale: Scleroderma renal crisis is treated with ACE inhibitor (typically captopril for rapid titration), even when AKI worsens transiently. ACEi blocks the renin-driven angiotensin II cascade that is the proximal driver of the malignant hypertension and microangiopathic haemolysis. Captopril is started 12.5 to 25 mg, uptitrated 6 to 8 hourly until BP is controlled; many patients still require dialysis but renal recovery is more likely with ACEi than without (eTG Rheumatology; Scleroderma Australia).

options.text: Urgent plasma exchange for thrombotic microangiopathy with adjunctive caplacizumab
options.rationale: ADAMTS13 activity of 65% rules out TTP (where activity is typically <10%). The microangiopathy in scleroderma renal crisis is secondary to malignant hypertension and renal endothelial injury, not deficient ADAMTS13. Plasma exchange does not reverse it; ACEi does (eTG; KHA-CARI).

options.text: High-dose IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days to suppress the underlying autoimmune disease
options.rationale: High-dose corticosteroid (especially >15 mg/day prednisolone) is a major risk factor for precipitating scleroderma renal crisis, particularly in anti-RNA polymerase III positive diffuse SSc. Escalating steroid worsens the condition. Steroids should be minimised and ACEi started (eTG; ARA).

options.text: Sublingual nifedipine 10 mg every 30 minutes until BP <160/100
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine causes unpredictable abrupt BP drops with risk of cerebral or coronary hypoperfusion and is contraindicated in hypertensive emergency. It also does not address the underlying renin-driven pathophysiology of scleroderma renal crisis (eTG Cardiovascular).

explanation.summary: Scleroderma renal crisis is a sudden malignant hypertension with AKI and microangiopathic haemolysis, typically in diffuse cutaneous SSc with anti-RNA polymerase III antibodies, often precipitated by glucocorticoid >15 mg/day prednisolone. Treatment is rapid-titration ACE inhibitor (captopril) even as creatinine rises further. ARBs are inferior. Survival and renal recovery depend on prompt ACEi.
key_points: Risk: diffuse SSc, anti-RNA pol III, recent steroid >15 mg/day
Triad: new malignant HTN + AKI + MAHA with normal ADAMTS13
Treatment: captopril uptitrated 6-8 hourly, accept transient creatinine rise
Avoid: high-dose steroid (precipitant), sublingual nifedipine, PLEX
Many need dialysis; recovery possible up to 18 months
explanation.pearls: Any new hypertension in a scleroderma patient on prednisolone is renal crisis until proven otherwise (eTG).

subtopic_detail: Hydroxychloroquine retinopathy - screening protocol and dose
stem: A 42-year-old woman with SLE diagnosed 18 months ago is reviewed in your medicine clinic. She is in clinical remission on hydroxychloroquine 400 mg daily and low-dose prednisolone 5 mg. Her weight is 58 kg, eGFR 78, and she has no liver disease. Past medications include tamoxifen 5 years ago for DCIS. Visual acuity is 6/6 bilaterally and she reports no visual symptoms. The previous registrar arranged baseline ophthalmology assessment at diagnosis which was normal. She asks how often she needs eye reviews and whether her dose is appropriate.

data_table.Background: SLE x 18 months, weight 58 kg, eGFR 78, prior tamoxifen 5 years ago.
data_table.Eyes: VA 6/6 bilateral, no symptoms, baseline ophthalmology normal at diagnosis.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate hydroxychloroquine dose and retinopathy screening plan?

options.text: Continue 400 mg daily; arrange repeat ophthalmology screening at 5 years from initiation, then annually
options.rationale: 400 mg in a 58 kg patient is 6.9 mg/kg, exceeding the 5 mg/kg actual body weight ceiling that minimises retinopathy risk per RANZCO and the American Academy of Ophthalmology. Dose reduction is required even if current screening interval is appropriate (RANZCO Position Statement).

options.text: Reduce to 300 mg daily (or alternate 200 mg/400 mg days); maintain annual ophthalmology screening from 5 years of cumulative use, earlier if risk factors are present
options.rationale: Dose ceiling is <=5 mg/kg actual body weight to minimise retinopathy risk; 290 mg for this 58 kg patient, so 300 mg daily or alternate-day dosing achieves the target. RANZCO and AAO recommend a baseline examination at initiation, then annual screening from 5 years of cumulative use. Earlier screening is indicated for tamoxifen co-exposure, CKD (eGFR <60), or daily dose >5 mg/kg. Her prior tamoxifen plus past over-dosing flags this patient for early annual screening.

options.text: Continue 400 mg daily; screen at 10 years from initiation given remission status
options.rationale: Clinical remission does not reduce retinopathy risk; cumulative dose does. 400 mg/day in a 58 kg patient is overdose. Screening at 10 years risks missing irreversible bull's-eye maculopathy. RANZCO recommends annual screening from 5 years (RANZCO).

options.text: Cease hydroxychloroquine entirely as she is in remission and switch to prednisolone alone
options.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine reduces SLE flares, organ damage and mortality; ceasing in remission predictably leads to flare. Steroid monotherapy increases cumulative steroid toxicity. The correct action is dose adjustment and continued screening, not withdrawal (eTG; ARA).

options.text: Continue 400 mg daily and rely on Amsler grid self-testing in place of formal ophthalmology review
options.rationale: Amsler grid is insensitive to early hydroxychloroquine retinopathy, which begins as parafoveal photoreceptor disruption detectable only with OCT, automated visual fields (10-2 or 24-2 with central testing) and fundus autofluorescence. Self-testing is not a substitute for formal annual screening (RANZCO).

explanation.summary: Hydroxychloroquine dose should not exceed 5 mg/kg actual body weight to minimise retinal toxicity. RANZCO recommends baseline ophthalmology at initiation, then annual screening from 5 years of cumulative use, earlier if dose >5 mg/kg, CKD eGFR <60, tamoxifen co-exposure or pre-existing macular disease. Modern screening combines OCT, automated 10-2 (or 24-2) visual fields and fundus autofluorescence; Amsler grid is inadequate.
key_points: Dose ceiling: 5 mg/kg actual body weight
Baseline ophthalmology at start; annual from year 5
Earlier annual screening: high dose, CKD, tamoxifen, macular disease
Screening tools: OCT + 10-2 visual fields + FAF, not Amsler
Hydroxychloroquine reduces SLE flares and damage; do not cease in remission
explanation.pearls: Tamoxifen co-exposure dramatically increases HCQ retinopathy risk - flag in clinic letters (RANZCO).

subtopic_detail: Lupus nephritis - class IV induction therapy
stem: A 26-year-old woman with SLE (positive ANA 1:640 homogeneous, anti-dsDNA 380 IU/mL, low C3 and C4) presents to your medicine clinic with 4 weeks of ankle swelling. She takes hydroxychloroquine 300 mg daily. Vital signs are BP 152/94, HR 84, afebrile. Examination shows malar rash, oral ulcers, pitting oedema to the knees and a clear chest. Bloods show haemoglobin 102, WCC 4.2, platelets 132, creatinine 124 (baseline 70 four months ago), albumin 22 g/L, ALT 32. Urinalysis 3+ protein, 2+ blood; spot urine protein:creatinine ratio 480 mg/mmol; urine microscopy red cell casts. C3 0.42 g/L (low), C4 0.07 g/L (low), anti-dsDNA 380 IU/mL. Renal biopsy reports class IV (diffuse proliferative) lupus nephritis with active lesions (NIH activity index 12/24) and no chronic damage. She has not previously been on cyclophosphamide or mycophenolate.

data_table.Bloods: Hb 102, WCC 4.2, plt 132, creatinine 124 (baseline 70), albumin 22, ALT 32.
data_table.Urine: 3+ protein, 2+ blood, RBC casts, uPCR 480 mg/mmol.
data_table.Serology: ANA 1:640, anti-dsDNA 380, C3 0.42 low, C4 0.07 low.
data_table.Biopsy: Class IV LN, NIH activity 12/24, no chronicity.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate induction regimen?

options.text: IV methylprednisolone 500 mg for 3 days then oral prednisolone, continue hydroxychloroquine, plus mycophenolate 2 to 3 g/day OR cyclophosphamide 500 mg IV fortnightly for 6 doses
options.rationale: KHA-CARI 2022 and EULAR/ERA-EDTA endorse pulsed glucocorticoid followed by oral prednisolone 0.5 to 0.8 mg/kg/day (reduced-dose taper) plus either mycophenolate mofetil 2 to 3 g/day or low-dose IV cyclophosphamide (Euro-Lupus 500 mg fortnightly x 6) as induction for proliferative class III/IV lupus nephritis. Hydroxychloroquine is continued. Belimumab or voclosporin add-on is increasingly used. MMF is preferred in women of reproductive age to preserve fertility.
source_refs: KHA-CARI Recommendations for the Management of Lupus Nephritis 2022

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day with azathioprine 2 mg/kg/day, continuing hydroxychloroquine
options.rationale: Azathioprine is a maintenance agent for lupus nephritis, not induction. Inducting class IV LN with azathioprine accepts ongoing glomerular damage and risks progression to ESKD (KHA-CARI 2022).
source_refs: KHA-CARI Recommendations for the Management of Lupus Nephritis 2022

options.text: Rituximab 1 g x 2 doses 2 weeks apart, plus oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day, ceasing hydroxychloroquine to avoid combined immunosuppression
options.rationale: Rituximab is considered for refractory or relapsing LN, not first-line induction (LUNAR trial did not meet primary endpoint). Ceasing hydroxychloroquine worsens outcomes in lupus and is contrary to all guidelines (KHA-CARI 2022; ARA).
source_refs: KHA-CARI Recommendations for the Management of Lupus Nephritis 2022

options.text: ACE inhibitor and statin alone, with deferred immunosuppression until creatinine doubles
options.rationale: Class IV LN with red cell casts and rising creatinine requires urgent immunosuppression. Waiting for creatinine doubling commits the patient to irreversible glomerular sclerosis. ACEi and statin are adjuncts, not substitutes (KHA-CARI).
source_refs: KHA-CARI Recommendations for the Management of Lupus Nephritis 2022

options.text: Plasma exchange daily for 7 days plus high-dose oral prednisolone without cytotoxic therapy
options.rationale: Plasma exchange has no proven benefit in lupus nephritis outside of co-existing TTP or anti-GBM. Used alone it does not address the underlying glomerular immune complex deposition (KHA-CARI 2022).
source_refs: KHA-CARI Recommendations for the Management of Lupus Nephritis 2022

explanation.summary: Proliferative lupus nephritis (class III/IV) is treated with pulsed glucocorticoid plus mycophenolate mofetil or low-dose IV cyclophosphamide (Euro-Lupus). Hydroxychloroquine is continued lifelong. Maintenance is mycophenolate or azathioprine for at least 3 years. Belimumab and voclosporin are emerging add-ons. ACEi/ARB and BP control to <130/80 are essential adjuncts.
key_points: Class III/IV LN: pulse steroid + MMF or low-dose IV cyclophosphamide
MMF preferred in fertility-sparing context
Hydroxychloroquine always continued
Maintenance: MMF or azathioprine >=3 years
ACEi/ARB and BP <130/80 mandatory adjuncts
explanation.pearls: Euro-Lupus 500 mg IV cyclophosphamide fortnightly x 6 is markedly less toxic than the older NIH regimen with equivalent renal outcomes (KHA-CARI 2022).

sources.label: KHA-CARI Recommendations for the Management of Lupus Nephritis 2022

stem: A 71-year-old man presents to your general practice with 5 weeks of bilateral shoulder and hip-girdle pain with morning stiffness lasting 2 hours. He has difficulty rising from a chair and combing his hair. He has lost 2 kg, has no fever, no headache, no jaw claudication, no visual symptoms and no rash. Examination shows tender shoulder and hip-girdle muscles with restricted active range of motion. There is no synovitis, no muscle weakness on confrontation testing, no temporal artery tenderness. Vital signs are HR 76, BP 138/82, afebrile. Bloods show haemoglobin 124, WCC 7.8, platelets 360, creatinine 84, ESR 68 mm/h, CRP 54, normal CK, normal TSH, normal calcium and PSA. Hand/shoulder X-rays show no erosions. RF and anti-CCP negative.

data_table.Symptoms: Bilateral shoulder/hip-girdle pain, morning stiffness 2 h, 5 weeks.
data_table.No: Fever, headache, jaw claudication, visual symptoms, rash, synovitis, weakness.
data_table.Bloods: Hb 124, WCC 7.8, plt 360, ESR 68, CRP 54, CK normal, TSH normal, Ca normal, PSA normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial treatment?

options.text: Prednisolone 15 mg orally daily, with calcium, vitamin D and bone-protection assessment, expecting marked symptomatic improvement within 1 week
options.rationale: EULAR/ACR and eTG recommend prednisolone 12.5 to 25 mg daily as the starting dose for PMR. Most patients improve within days; lack of >=75% response by 1 week should prompt diagnostic re-evaluation (GCA, RA, inflammatory myopathy, late-onset spondyloarthropathy). Co-prescribe calcium 1.3 g, vitamin D, and assess fracture risk for bisphosphonate therapy (PBS criteria met when prednisolone >=7.5 mg/day for >=3 months).

options.text: Prednisolone 1 mg/kg orally daily as for GCA, with high-dose pulse if visual symptoms occur
options.rationale: PMR without cranial symptoms responds to 12.5 to 25 mg/day; using GCA doses (60 to 80 mg) over-treats and dramatically increases steroid toxicity (osteoporosis, diabetes, infection). High-dose pulse is reserved for ophthalmic or neurological GCA presentations (eTG).

options.text: Methotrexate 15 mg weekly as first-line monotherapy with folic acid
options.rationale: Methotrexate is a steroid-sparing agent considered for relapsing PMR or unacceptable steroid toxicity, not initial monotherapy. PMR is exquisitely steroid-responsive and that response is itself diagnostic (eTG).

options.text: NSAIDs (celecoxib 200 mg twice daily) as first-line, reserving steroid for non-response
options.rationale: NSAIDs are generally ineffective for PMR and risk gastric, renal and cardiovascular harm in this age group. Steroid is the standard first-line therapy (eTG).

options.text: Tocilizumab 8 mg/kg IV monthly as first-line biologic therapy
options.rationale: Tocilizumab is currently used for steroid-refractory or relapsing PMR (and is the standard add-on for GCA), not as initial therapy. PBS subsidy is for GCA, not PMR alone (PBS).

explanation.summary: PMR is a clinical diagnosis in patients >50 with new bilateral shoulder/hip-girdle pain, morning stiffness >45 min, elevated CRP/ESR, and exclusion of mimics (RA, inflammatory myopathy, hypothyroidism, malignancy, late-onset spondyloarthropathy). Treatment is prednisolone 12.5 to 25 mg/day with rapid symptomatic response. Steroid-sparing methotrexate or tocilizumab for relapse or toxicity. Always screen for GCA at every review.
key_points: PMR starting dose: prednisolone 12.5-25 mg/day
Rapid response (within 1 week) is diagnostic and required
Always screen for coexisting GCA at every review
Bone protection from initiation; bisphosphonate if Tx >=3 months
MTX or tocilizumab for relapse or steroid toxicity
explanation.pearls: Failure to respond to 25 mg prednisolone within 1 week means it is not PMR - reconsider RA, inflammatory myopathy or malignancy (eTG).

subtopic_detail: IgA vasculitis adult - renal indication for biopsy
stem: A 34-year-old man presents to your general medicine clinic 3 weeks after an upper respiratory tract infection with a palpable purpuric rash on both lower legs and buttocks, bilateral ankle and knee arthralgia, and crampy abdominal pain. Vital signs are HR 82, BP 138/86, afebrile. Examination confirms non-blanching palpable purpura, no synovitis, soft non-tender abdomen, no peripheral oedema. Bloods show haemoglobin 142, WCC 9.6, platelets 320, creatinine 92 (baseline 78), CRP 28. Coagulation profile normal. Urinalysis shows 2+ blood, 2+ protein. Spot urine protein:creatinine ratio 105 mg/mmol. Microscopy shows dysmorphic red cells without casts. Throat swab negative. ASOT normal. ANCA negative.

data_table.Examination: Palpable purpura legs/buttocks, no synovitis, soft abdomen.
data_table.Bloods: Hb 142, WCC 9.6, plt 320, creatinine 92 (baseline 78), CRP 28, coags normal.
data_table.Urine: 2+ blood, 2+ protein, uPCR 105 mg/mmol, dysmorphic RBCs no casts.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer for urgent renal biopsy now, as proteinuria of this severity in adult IgA vasculitis warrants histological confirmation and guides immunosuppression
options.rationale: Adult IgA vasculitis carries substantially higher renal risk than the childhood form. KDIGO 2024 and KHA-CARI guidance recommend renal biopsy in adults with uPCR >100 mg/mmol (or proteinuria persisting >4 weeks), creatinine rise, or nephritic features, because crescentic IgA nephritis requires immunosuppression. His uPCR of 105 mg/mmol and creatinine creep meet criteria. Biopsy distinguishes those who need corticosteroids/cyclophosphamide from those manageable with ACEi alone.

options.text: Reassure, manage symptomatically with paracetamol, and review in 6 weeks for spontaneous resolution
options.rationale: Childhood IgA vasculitis is largely self-limiting, but adult disease has a ~30% risk of CKD or ESKD. Significant proteinuria with rising creatinine is not safe to observe for 6 weeks without renal characterisation (KHA-CARI).

options.text: Start empirical oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day without biopsy and review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Empirical high-dose steroid commits the patient to side effects without confirming histology, and undertreats severe crescentic disease that may require steroid plus cyclophosphamide. Steroid for cutaneous and arthralgic symptoms alone is also non-evidence-based (KDIGO 2024).

options.text: Start an ACE inhibitor and review urine protein in 12 weeks before deciding on biopsy
options.rationale: ACEi is appropriate adjunct therapy but does not characterise the histological severity. Significant proteinuria with creatinine rise in adult IgA vasculitis is an early-biopsy indication; a 12-week observation can miss crescentic GN window for treatment (KHA-CARI).

options.text: Send a skin biopsy of a purpuric lesion for direct immunofluorescence as definitive testing
options.rationale: Skin biopsy with IgA-dominant immune complex deposition supports IgA vasculitis diagnostically but does not characterise renal involvement or stratify treatment. With significant proteinuria, renal biopsy is the indicated next step (KHA-CARI; KDIGO).

explanation.summary: Adult IgA vasculitis differs from the childhood form in carrying significant renal risk (~30% CKD/ESKD). Renal biopsy is indicated for: uPCR >100 mg/mmol persisting beyond 4 weeks, creatinine rise, or nephritic features. Histology determines need for immunosuppression: crescentic disease is treated with steroid +/- cyclophosphamide, while milder mesangioproliferative disease is managed with ACEi/ARB and BP control. ASOT is checked to exclude post-streptococcal GN.
key_points: Adult IgA vasculitis: 30% develop CKD
Biopsy if uPCR >100 mg/mmol, AKI, nephritic features
Histology determines immunosuppression intensity
ACEi/ARB and BP control for all with proteinuria
Childhood form usually self-limiting; adult form not
explanation.pearls: An adult with the classic IgA vasculitis tetrad (purpura, arthralgia, abdominal pain, glomerulonephritis) and persistent proteinuria needs prompt renal biopsy (KHA-CARI).

subtopic_detail: Antiphospholipid syndrome - obstetric pathway and aspirin/LMWH
stem: A 32-year-old woman is referred to your medicine clinic at 7 weeks gestation. She has had three consecutive first-trimester miscarriages between 6 and 9 weeks. She has no history of thrombosis. She is otherwise well, not on any medications, BMI 26. Investigations 14 weeks ago and again 12 weeks later show persistently positive lupus anticoagulant on two occasions, IgG anticardiolipin antibody 92 GPL (positive >40), and beta-2-glycoprotein-1 IgG 78 (positive >40). ANA is negative. Bloods show haemoglobin 132, WCC 8.2, platelets 244, normal coagulation studies, normal renal and liver function. She is normotensive.

data_table.Serology: Lupus anticoagulant + x 2 (12 wks apart), aCL IgG 92 (>40), b2GP1 IgG 78 (>40), ANA neg.
data_table.Bloods: Hb 132, WCC 8.2, plt 244, normal coags, normal UEC/LFTs.
data_table.Background: BMI 26, no thrombosis, no current medications.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management for this pregnancy?

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg orally daily commenced now, continued throughout pregnancy
options.rationale: Aspirin alone is insufficient for women meeting obstetric APS criteria with three or more first-trimester miscarriages. Combined low-dose aspirin plus prophylactic LMWH improves live birth rates over aspirin alone (RANZCOG; ARA). Aspirin monotherapy is reserved for asymptomatic aPL carriers (no obstetric or thrombotic history) requiring primary prevention.

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg orally daily plus prophylactic-dose enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily, continued throughout pregnancy and 6 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: Obstetric APS (>=3 unexplained first-trimester losses, or fetal loss >=10 weeks, or severe pre-eclampsia/placental insufficiency <34 weeks, with persistent aPL on testing 12 weeks apart) is treated with low-dose aspirin plus prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy. LMWH is continued for 6 weeks postpartum due to venous thromboembolism risk. RANZCOG and ARA guidance align (Sapporo criteria).

options.text: Therapeutic-dose enoxaparin 1 mg/kg subcutaneously twice daily and warfarin to INR 2 to 3 after the first trimester
options.rationale: Therapeutic anticoagulation is reserved for thrombotic APS (prior venous or arterial event), not obstetric APS without thrombosis. Warfarin is teratogenic and contraindicated in pregnancy. This regimen over-treats and exposes to warfarin embryopathy (RANZCOG).

options.text: Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg twice daily as monotherapy throughout pregnancy
options.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine may be an adjunct in refractory obstetric APS or in those with concurrent SLE, but is not first-line monotherapy. Standard care is aspirin + prophylactic LMWH (RANZCOG; ARA).

options.text: Prednisolone 20 mg orally daily plus IVIG infusion 1 g/kg monthly
options.rationale: Glucocorticoids and IVIG were trialled historically with negative results and added maternal morbidity (gestational diabetes, hypertension, PPROM). They are not standard of care for obstetric APS and may be considered only in refractory cases under specialist supervision (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Obstetric APS is defined by clinical criteria (>=3 unexplained first-trimester losses, or fetal loss >=10 weeks, or severe early pre-eclampsia/placental insufficiency <34 weeks) plus persistent laboratory criteria (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin IgG/IgM, or beta-2-glycoprotein-1 IgG/IgM) confirmed 12 weeks apart. Treatment is low-dose aspirin plus prophylactic LMWH from confirmation of pregnancy until 6 weeks postpartum. Thrombotic APS requires therapeutic anticoagulation; warfarin is the agent outside pregnancy. DOACs are inferior to warfarin for triple-positive APS (TRAPS trial).
key_points: Obstetric APS = clinical criteria + persistent aPL 12 weeks apart
Treatment: aspirin 100 mg + prophylactic LMWH through pregnancy + 6 wks postpartum
Thrombotic APS = warfarin INR 2-3 (outside pregnancy); DOACs inferior
Hydroxychloroquine: adjunct in refractory cases or with SLE
Avoid: steroid/IVIG (not standard), warfarin in pregnancy
explanation.pearls: Triple-positive APS (LA + aCL + b2GP1) has the highest event rate; do not use DOACs (TRAPS).

subtopic_detail: Polyarteritis nodosa - hepatitis B association and treatment
stem: A 47-year-old man presents to your hospital clinic with 8 weeks of weight loss (8 kg), fevers, severe testicular pain, livedo reticularis on the lower limbs, hypertension (BP 178/108) and a new right foot drop. He emigrated from Vietnam 20 years ago. Bloods show haemoglobin 102, WCC 13.4, platelets 488, creatinine 138 (baseline 80), ALT 142, AST 168, GGT 240, ALP 168, albumin 32, CRP 96. Hepatitis B surface antigen is positive, HBeAg positive, HBV DNA 4.6 x10^7 IU/mL, anti-HCV negative, HIV negative, ANCA negative (both PR3 and MPO), ANA negative, cryoglobulins negative, complement normal. Urinalysis shows no blood, no protein. Mesenteric angiography demonstrates multiple small renal and mesenteric arterial microaneurysms; renal biopsy is deferred given imaging findings.

data_table.Examination: Livedo reticularis lower limbs, right foot drop, testicular tenderness.
data_table.Bloods: Hb 102, WCC 13.4, plt 488, creatinine 138 (baseline 80), ALT 142, AST 168, GGT 240, ALP 168, albumin 32, CRP 96.
data_table.Serology: HBsAg+, HBeAg+, HBV DNA 4.6x10^7 IU/mL, anti-HCV neg, HIV neg, ANCA neg, ANA neg, cryoglobulins neg.
data_table.Imaging: Mesenteric angio: multiple renal and mesenteric microaneurysms.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate treatment combination?

options.text: Prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day plus cyclophosphamide 2 mg/kg/day orally for 12 months
options.rationale: In HBV-associated PAN, prolonged cyclophosphamide and ongoing steroid sustain viral replication and accelerate liver damage. The Guillevin protocol uses a short steroid course followed by plasma exchange and antiviral therapy rather than prolonged cytotoxic immunosuppression (ASHM; eTG Rheumatology).

options.text: Short course of high-dose corticosteroid (e.g. prednisolone 1 mg/kg tapered over 2 weeks) plus plasma exchange, plus immediate antiviral therapy with entecavir or tenofovir
options.rationale: HBV-associated PAN is treated with a short induction of corticosteroid (to control the vasculitic inflammation), plasma exchange (to clear immune complexes), and prompt nucleos(t)ide antiviral therapy (entecavir or tenofovir) to suppress HBV replication, which is the proximal driver. Prolonged cyclophosphamide is avoided as it perpetuates viraemia and liver injury. This Guillevin-protocol approach is endorsed by ASHM and eTG Rheumatology.

options.text: Rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly for 4 weeks plus prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day
options.rationale: Rituximab is the standard induction for ANCA-associated vasculitis but is not standard for classic medium-vessel PAN, and B-cell depletion increases HBV reactivation risk dramatically. Without confirmed efficacy and with substantial harm risk, this is the wrong choice (ASHM).

options.text: Pegylated interferon-alpha monotherapy for 48 weeks with prednisolone 60 mg daily
options.rationale: Pegylated interferon was used historically for HBV-associated vasculitis but has largely been superseded by nucleos(t)ide analogues (entecavir, tenofovir) which give faster, more reliable viral suppression with fewer adverse effects. Long high-dose prednisolone alongside interferon increases harm without diagnostic benefit (ASHM).

options.text: Prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day monotherapy with antihypertensive optimisation, deferring antiviral until vasculitis resolves
options.rationale: Deferring antiviral therapy in HBV-associated PAN allows ongoing viral replication, immune complex deposition and disease progression; high-dose monotherapy steroid alone permits HBV flare. Antiviral suppression is the disease-modifying intervention and should not wait (ASHM).

explanation.summary: Polyarteritis nodosa is a necrotising medium-vessel vasculitis with multisystem involvement (renal, mesenteric, cutaneous livedo, testicular, neuropathy) and characteristic angiographic microaneurysms. Glomerulonephritis is notably absent. Historically ~30% of cases were HBV-associated; this has fallen with vaccination but remains relevant in patients from HBV-endemic areas. HBV-associated PAN is treated with brief corticosteroid, plasma exchange, and immediate nucleos(t)ide antiviral therapy (entecavir or tenofovir). Idiopathic PAN is treated with corticosteroid plus cyclophosphamide.
key_points: PAN: medium-vessel, microaneurysms, no glomerulonephritis
Always test HBV in newly diagnosed PAN
HBV-PAN: short steroid + PLEX + entecavir/tenofovir
Idiopathic PAN: steroid + cyclophosphamide
Avoid prolonged immunosuppression in HBV-PAN
explanation.pearls: The combination of livedo, mononeuritis multiplex, testicular pain and renal microaneurysms with normal urine sediment is classic for PAN (eTG).
